name: latex-rubber

metadata.name: latex-rubber
metadata.summary: an automated system for building LaTeX documents

documentation:
# This is Rubber.

Rubber is a building system for LaTeX documents. It is based on a routine that
runs just as many compilations as necessary and runs companion programs like
BibTeX and Makeindex when needed. The module system provides a great
flexibility that virtually allows support for any package with no user
intervention, as well as pre- and post-processing of the document. A good
number of standard packages are supported, including graphics/graphicx with
automatic conversion between various graphics formats and Metapost
compilation. The associated tool "rubber-info" extracts information, such as
dependency relations or post-compilation diagnostics.


# Installation

Running Rubber just requires Python 3.8.  Of course it won't
be of much use without a working LaTeX environment (Rubber is known to work on
TeXLive and VTeX on various flavors of Unix including Darwin and Cygwin, any
feedback is welcome about other systems).

For compilation, you will need the Python Distutils, which are usually included
in development packages (in Debian, this is the python3-dev package).

To build the documentation, you need texinfo (Debian package: texinfo). Note
that all the documentation formats are disabled by default.

To compile and install Rubber, just follow the usual procedure:

```console
# python3 setup.py --help
# python3 setup.py install
# python3 setup.py clean --all
```

Some useful options to setup.py include:

Disabling info docs:

```
# python3 setup.py build --info=False install
```

and similar for `--html`, `--man`, `--pdf`.

Changing the installation path for manpages:

```console
# python3 setup.py install --mandir=/path/to/man/pages
```

Installing to a staging directory instead of the root/prefix:

```console
# python3 setup.py install --root=/staging/directory
```

Note that if you need build and install to be two different steps
(for example when building packages for distribution purposes),
Python's distutils will forget about any "build" options, and re-build
with default options during the "install" stage.  This is worrysome if
you'd like not to build some of the documentation.  It is then best
to make options permanent by putting them info a setup.cfg file.  For
example:

```
[build]
man = 1
html = 0
pdf = 0
info = 0
txt = 0
[install]
prefix  = /usr
```

Finally, invoke

```console
# python3 setup.py build
# python3 setup.py install --root=/staging/directory
```


# PEP 517 Build

The newfangled build process for Python processes is specified in PEP 517.
There's initial support for this, but the packages will not include the
documentation.

The development flow is as follows:

## Setup a virtual environment, using a Python version of your choice:

```console
# python3 -m venv .venv
```


## Activate the virtual environment, and install the requirements:

```console
# source .venv/bin/activate
(.venv) # pip install -U pip setuptools wheel
(.venv) # pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
```

This is the virtual environment setup, it only needs to be done once. If the
requirements change, rerun this latest step.

To create a PEP 571-compatible sdist and wheel, run:

```console
# make build-wheel
```

This uses the "build" module to create the files in the "dist" folder. These
are the files to be uploaded to PyPI.


# PyPI Release

To publish the PEP 517 

See https://twine.readthedocs.io for how to setup twine regarding credentials.

After building the sdist and wheel, upload them to PyPI by using:

```console
# make publish-wheel
```


# Usage

As civility requires, saying `rubber --help` and `rubber-info --help` provides
a short description of the command line's syntax. The included manual pages
(available in English and French) and Texinfo documentation contain more
precise usage information.


# Known Bugs

Rubber is generally working fine, though there are some known issues.

1) Rubber tries to do too much.  Rubber attempts to provide a one-stop solution
to compiling a TeX document, and run a lot of related software as needed.
This approach is fragile for three reasons: a) It does so by parsing the .tex
file itself, and all included files, and discovering any related input files.
TeX is a hard language to parse correctly without embedding a full TeX
interpreter, which rubber does not do.  b) To do its work, Rubber needs to be
taught about every version of every package in the TeX ecosystem in order to
gauge its impact on the compilation of a TeX document.  It needs to know the
preferences of any TeX compiler with regards to image formats, search paths
etc.  All this information is hard to keep up to date.  c) In some cases (like
image conversion), it needs to outright guess what the user intends to do, and
may in fact guess incorrectly.
In a future release, some of these features may be taken out in favor of more
modern ways to accomplish the same thing, with to goal to render Rubber simpler
and more robust.  One might want to make use of the -recorder feature (.fls)
for example instead of attempting to read the same information from the
human-readable log file.

2) The codebase has been cleaned up considerably, has been converted to Python3
and is generally in a sane state.  Nevertheless, it has been written over a
number of years, and some features would be implemented differently or skipped
altogether if a rewrite were attempted (e.g. the onchange mechanism, modules,
...).

3) In some cases, Rubber will trigger a recompile that is arguably unnecessary.
Rubber tries to err on the side of caution here.


# Author

Rubber was originally written by Emmanuel Beffara <emmanuel@beffara.org>.
It is currently maintained by Florian Schmaus <flo@geekplace.eu>,
Sebastian Kapfer <sebastian.kapfer@fau.de> and Nicolas Boulenguez
<nicolas@debian.org>.

Its homepage can be found at https://gitlab.com/latex-rubber/rubber

Thanks to all those who provided testing, comments and suggestions, and
re-thanks to those who wrote patches and bugfixes.

Any kind of feedback is appreciated, in order to make this program as useful
and robust as possible.


# License

Rubber is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
(at your option) any later version.

This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
GNU General Public License for more details.

You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
along with this program.  If not, see <https://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.

```
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
```

modules.name: rubber.converters.literate
documentation:
Literate programming support for Rubber.

Nodes to make the main TeX file.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
Sebastian Kapfer <sebastian.kapfer@fau.de>
vim: noet:ts=4

modules.name: rubber.converters.eps_gz
documentation: Extraction of bounding box information from gzipped PostScript figures.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

methods.name: rubber.converters.eps_gz.Dep.run
documentation:
This method reads the source file (which is supposed to be a
gzip-compressed PostScript file) until it finds a line that contains a
bounding box indication. Then it creates the target file with this
single line.

modules.name: rubber.converters.shell
documentation:
Generic shell conversion rule.

The action of this rule is defined by variables specified in the rule file:
- "command" is the command line, it is split the same way as directives,
   other variables are substituted,
- "source" is the input file name,
- "target" is the output file name.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2005

modules.name: rubber.converters.fig2dev
documentation: Conversion of XFig graphics into various formats.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

modules.name: rubber.converters.mpost
documentation:
Metapost support for Rubber.

The module parses input files for dependencies and does some checkings on
Metapost's log files after the process. Is it enough?
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
vim: noet:ts=4
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

classes.name: rubber.converters.mpost.MPLogCheck

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.MPLogCheck.read
documentation:
The read() method in LogCheck checks that the log is produced by TeX,
here we check that it is produced by MetaPost.

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.MPLogCheck.continued
documentation:
Messages in Metapost logs are broken at 79 characters per line, except
in some cases where there are lines of this length that are not
continued...

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.MPLogCheck.get_errors
documentation:
Parse the Metapost log file for errors. The file has the same form as
a TeX log file, so the parser for TeX logs is used. The special case
is that of TeX errors in Metapost labels, which requires parsing
another TeX log file.

This class adapats the LogCheck class from the main program to the case of
MetaPost log files, which are very similar to TeX log files.

classes.name: rubber.converters.mpost.Dep

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.Dep.include
documentation:
This function tries to find a specified MetaPost source (currently all
in the same directory), appends its actual name to the list, and
parses it to find recursively included files.

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.Dep.run
documentation:
Run Metapost from the source file's directory, so that figures are put
next to their source file.

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.Dep.get_errors
documentation: Report the errors from the last compilation.

methods.name: rubber.converters.mpost.Dep.clean
documentation:
This method removes all the files that the Metapost compilation may
have created. It is required because the compilation may produce more
files than just the figures used by the document.

This class represents dependency nodes for MetaPost figures. The __init__
method simply creates one node for the figures and one leaf node for all
sources.

modules.name: rubber.converters.compressor
documentation: Compressing the output of Rubber.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Nicolas Boulenguez 2015

modules.name: rubber.biblio
documentation: Bibliographies (Biber and BibTeX).
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
Sebastian Kapfer <sebastian.kapfer@fau.de> 2015.
based on code by Sebastian Reichel and others.
vim: noet:ts=4

methods.name: rubber.biblio.BibToolDep.get_errors
documentation: Read the log file, identify error messages and report them.

classes.name: rubber.biblio.BibTeXDep

methods.name: rubber.biblio.BibTeXDep.__init__
documentation:
Initialise the bibiliography for the given document. The base name is
that of the aux file from which citations are taken.

methods.name: rubber.biblio.BibTeXDep.do_crossrefs
documentation: The following method are used to specify the various datafiles that
BibTeX uses.

methods.name: rubber.biblio.BibTeXDep.hook_bibliographystyle
documentation:
Define the bibliography style used. This method is called when
\bibliographystyle is found. If the style file is found in the
current directory, it is considered a dependency.
This class represents a single bibliography for a document.

modules.name: rubber.dvip_tool
documentation:
Abstract class providing a common implementation for
rubber.latex_modules.dvips and rubber.latex_modules.dvipdfm.

PostScript/PDF generation through dvips/odvips/dvipdfm with Rubber.

When the name of the main compiler is "Omega" (instead of "TeX" for
instance), then "odvips" is used instead of "dvips".
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
(c) Sebastian Kapfer 2015
vim:et:ts=4

modules.name: rubber.tex
documentation:
General-purpose classes for reading TeX code.
Classes and functions from this module can be used without Rubber.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2008
vim: noet:ts=4

functions.name: rubber.tex.re_set
documentation:
Returns a string that contains a regular expression matching a given set
of characters, or its complement if the optional argument is true. The set
must not be empty.

functions.name: rubber.tex.parse_string
documentation: Factory function for parsing TeX code from a string.

classes.name: rubber.tex.Position
documentation: A class to represent positions in a source file.

classes.name: rubber.tex.Token
documentation:
The class used to represent tokens. Objects contain a catcode, a value
(for control sequences) and the raw text that represents them in the input
file.

classes.name: rubber.tex.TokenList

methods.name: rubber.tex.TokenList.raw_text
documentation:
Return the textual representation of the token list by concatenating
the raw text of the tokens.

This class represents a token list. It behaves as a standard list with
some extra functionality.

classes.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.catcode
documentation: Return the catcode of a character.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.put_token
documentation: Put back a token in the input.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.put_list
documentation: Put back a token list in the input.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.peek_token
documentation: Return the next token that will be read without updating the state.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_token
documentation: Get the next token from the input and update the math mode.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.__iter__
documentation:
Return an iterator over all tokens in the input. The EOF token is not
returned by this iterator.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.skip_space
documentation: Skip white space in the input.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_group
documentation:
Get the list of tokens up to the next closing brace, and drop the
closing brace.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_group_text
documentation:
Get the list of tokens up to the next closing brace, and drop the
closing brace. Return the list as a string.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_argument
documentation:
Get a macro argument from the input text. Returns a token list with
the value of the argument, with surrounding braces removed if
relevant.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_argument_text
documentation:
Get a macro argument from the input text. Returns a string with
the text of the argument, with surrounding braces removed if
relevant.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_latex_optional
documentation:
Check if a LaTeX-style optional argument is present. If such an
argument is present, return it as a token list, otherwise return None.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_latex_optional_text
documentation:
Check if a LaTeX-style optional argument is present. If such an
argument is present, return it as text, otherwise return None.

methods.name: rubber.tex.ParserBase.get_latex_star
documentation:
Check if the command is a starred one.  If so, eat the star,
and return True.  Otherwise, return False.

This is the base class for parsers. It holds state information like
catcodes, handles the push-back buffer, and leaves it to derived classes
to actually read tokens, using the "read_token" method. This class also
provides high-level functionality like getting macro arguments.

classes.name: rubber.tex.Parser

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.__init__
documentation:
Initialise the parser with a file as input.
If 'input' is None, then input can only be provided by
the 'put_token' and 'put_list' methods.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.read_line
documentation:
Reads a line of input and sets the attribute 'line' with it. Returns
True if reading succeeded and False if it failed.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.read_char
documentation:
Get the next character from the input and its catcode (without parsing
control sequences).

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.read_token
documentation: Get the next token from the input.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.re_cat
documentation:
Returns a regular expression that maches characters whose category is
in given list.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.re_nocat
documentation:
Returns a regular expression that maches characters whose category is
not in a given list.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.set_hooks
documentation: Define the set of hooks for 'next_hook'.

methods.name: rubber.tex.Parser.next_hook
documentation:
Ignore input until the next control sequence from the set defined by
'set_hooks'. Returns the associated token, or the EOF token if no hook
was found.

A parser for TeX code that reads its input from a file object.

The class also provides a hook feature: the method 'set_hooks' declares a
set of control sequence names, and the method 'next_hook' parses the input
until it finds a control sequence from this set, ignoring all other
tokens. This advantage of this method is that is is much faster than
reading tokens one by one.

modules.name: rubber
documentation: SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
These exceptions are caught by cmdline.py,
which selects an exit status accordingly.

classes.name: rubber.GenericError
documentation: errors running LaTeX, finding essential files, etc.

classes.name: rubber.SyntaxError
documentation: signal invalid Rubber command-line

modules.name: rubber.util
documentation:
This module contains utility functions and classes used by the main system and
by the modules for various tasks.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
vim:noet:ts=4

functions.name: rubber.util._
documentation: -- Message writers --{{{1
The function `_' is defined here to prepare for internationalization.

functions.name: rubber.util._format
documentation:
Format the given text into a proper error message, with file and line
information in the standard format. Position information is taken from
the dictionary given as first argument.

functions.name: rubber.util.parse_keyval
documentation:
Parse a list of 'key=value' pairs, with the syntax used in LaTeX's
standard 'keyval' package. The value returned is simply a dictionary that
contains all definitions found in the string. For keys without a value,
the dictionary associates the value None.
If str is None, consider it as empty.

functions.name: rubber.util.match_brace
documentation:
Split the string at the first closing brace such that the extracted prefix
is balanced with respect to braces. The return value is a pair. If the
adequate closing brace is found, the pair contains the prefix before the
brace and the suffix after the brace (not containing the brace). If no
adequate brace is found, return the whole string as prefix and an empty
string as suffix.

functions.name: rubber.util.prog_available
documentation:
Test whether the specified program is available in the current path, and
return its actual path if it is found, or None.

functions.name: rubber.util.parse_line
documentation:
Decompose a string into a list of elements. The elements are separated by
spaces, single and double quotes allow escaping of spaces (and quotes).
Elements can contain variable references with the syntax '$VAR' (with only
letters in the name) or '${VAR}'.

If the argument 'dict' is defined, it is considered as a hash containing
the values of the variables. If it is None, elements with variables are
replaced by sequences of litteral strings or names, as follows:
    parse_line(" foo  bar${xy}quux toto  ")
        --> ["foo", ["'bar", "$xy", "'quux"], "toto"]

functions.name: rubber.util.explode_path
documentation: Parse an environment variable into a list of paths, and return it as an array.

functions.name: rubber.util.find_resource
documentation:
find the indicated file, mimicking what latex would do:
tries adding a suffix such as ".bib", or looking in the specified paths.
if unsuccessful, returns None.

functions.name: rubber.util.execute
documentation:
Silently execute an external program. The `prog' argument is the list
of arguments for the program, `prog[0]' is the program name. The `env'
argument is a dictionary with definitions that should be added to the
environment when running the program. The standard output is passed
line by line to the `out' function (or discarded by default).

modules.name: rubber.module_interface
documentation:
This module defines the common interface for all rubber modules.

Most of them are implemented as Python modules under
rubber.latex_modules.  Each one contains a subclass of
rubber.module_interface.Module that will be instantiated once when the
plugin is loaded by rubber.converters.latex.

rubber.converters.latex also declares a subclass that is instantiated
each time a .rub file is read.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
module_interface.py, part of Rubber building system for LaTeX documents..
Copyright (C) 2015-2015 Nicolas Boulenguez <nicolas.boulenguez@free.fr>
This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify
it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
the Free Software Foundation; either version 2 of the License, or
(at your option) any later version.
This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
GNU General Public License for more details.
You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
along with this program.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.

classes.name: rubber.module_interface.Module

methods.name: rubber.module_interface.Module.pre_compile
documentation:
This method is called before the first LaTeX compilation. It is
supposed to build any file that LaTeX would require to compile the
document correctly. The method must return true on success.

methods.name: rubber.module_interface.Module.post_compile
documentation:
This method is called after each LaTeX compilation. It is supposed to
process the compilation results and possibly request a new
compilation. The method must return true on success.

methods.name: rubber.module_interface.Module.clean
documentation:
Remove additional files for this LaTeX module.
Nothing recursive happens here.
Files registered as products are removed by rubber.clean ().

methods.name: rubber.module_interface.Module.command
documentation:
This is called when a directive for the module is found in the source.
We treat syntax errors in the directive as fatal, aborting the run.

The _dep parameter is an implementation detail and must not
be used outside the latex_modules subdirectory.
When absent, self.dep is tried.

methods.name: rubber.module_interface.Module.get_errors
documentation:
This is called if something has failed during an operation performed
by this module. The method returns a generator with items of the same
form as in LaTeXDep.get_errors.

This is the base class for modules. Each module should define a class
named 'Module' that derives from this one. The default implementation
provides all required methods with no effects.

The constructor is mandatory. Its profile must be
def __init__ (self, document, opt):

'document' is the compiling environment (an instance of
converters.latex.LaTeXDep)

'opt' describes the option given to the macro that caused
the module to load.

modules.name: rubber.depend
documentation: This module contains code for handling dependency graphs.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later

functions.name: rubber.depend.clean_all_products
documentation: Clean all products of all recipes.

classes.name: rubber.depend.Node

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.__init__
documentation:
The node registers itself in the dependency set,
and if a given depedency is not known in the set, a leaf node is made
for it.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.all_leaves
documentation: Show sources that are not produced.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.add_source
documentation:
Register a new source for this node. If the source is unknown, a leaf
node is made for it.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.remove_source
documentation: Remove a source for this node.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.products
documentation:
An iterable with all all products for this recipe.
This function is not efficient, but called only once by
cmdline.py with a specific command-line option.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.add_product
documentation: Register a new product for this node.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.replace_product
documentation: Trick for latex.py

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.make
documentation:
Make the destination file. This recursively makes all dependencies,
then compiles the target if dependencies were modified. The return
value is
- False when nothing had to be done
- True when something was recompiled (among all dependencies)
MakeError is raised in case of error.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.run
documentation:
This method is called when a node has to be (re)built. It is supposed
to rebuild the files of this node, returning true on success and false
on failure. It must be redefined by derived classes.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.get_errors
documentation:
Report the errors that caused the failure of the last call to run, as
an iterable object.

methods.name: rubber.depend.Node.clean
documentation:
Remove additional files for this recipe.
Nothing recursive happens here.
Files registered as products are removed by rubber.clean ().

This is the base class to represent dependency nodes. It provides the base
functionality of date checking and recursive making, supposing the
existence of a method `run()' in the object.

classes.name: rubber.depend.Shell
documentation: This class specializes Node for generating files using shell commands.

classes.name: rubber.depend.Pipe
documentation:
This class specializes Node for generating files using the stdout of shell commands.
The 'product' will receive the stdout of 'command'.

modules.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1
documentation:
LaTeX document building system for Rubber.

This module contains all the code in Rubber that actually does the job of
building a LaTeX document from start to finish.
This file is part of Rubber and thus covered by the GPL
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
(c) Sebastian Kapfer 2015
vim: noet:ts=4

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.Modules

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.Modules.__getitem__
documentation: Return the module object of the given name.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.Modules.__contains__
documentation: Check if a given module is loaded.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.Modules.register
documentation:
Attempt to register a module with the specified name. If the module is
already loaded, do nothing. If it is found and not yet loaded, then
load it, initialise it (using the context passed as optional argument)
and run any delayed commands for it.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.Modules.command
documentation:
Send a command to a particular module. If this module is not loaded,
store the command so that it will be sent when the module is register.

This class gathers all operations related to the management of modules.
The modules are searched for first in the current directory, then as
scripts in the 'modules' directory in the program's data directort, then
as a Python module in the package `rubber.latex'.
----  Module handler  ----{{{1

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.__init__
documentation: -- Initialization {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.readlog
documentation:
Read the specified log file, checking that it was produced by the
right compiler. Returns False if the log file is invalid or does not
exist.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.errors
documentation: Returns true if there was an error during the compilation.
-- Process information {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.continued
documentation:
Check if a line in the log is continued on the next line. This is
needed because TeX breaks messages at 79 characters per line. We make
this into a method because the test is slightly different in Metapost.
-- Information extraction {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.parse
documentation:
Parse the log file for relevant information. The named arguments are
booleans that indicate which information should be extracted:
- errors: all errors
- boxes: bad boxes
- refs: warnings about references
- warnings: all other warnings
The function returns a generator. Each generated item is a dictionary
that contains (some of) the following entries:
- kind: the kind of information ("error", "box", "ref", "warning")
- text: the text of the error or warning
- code: the piece of code that caused an error
- file, line, last, pkg: as used by Message.format_pos.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.update_file
documentation:
Parse the given line of log file for file openings and closings and
update the list `stack'. Newly opened files are at the end, therefore
stack[1] is the main source while stack[-1] is the current one. The
first element, stack[0], contains the value None for errors that may
happen outside the source. Return the last file from which text was
read (the new stack top, or the one before the last closing
parenthesis).

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LogCheck.update_page
documentation:
Parse the given line and return the number of the page that is being
built after that line, assuming the current page before the line was
`before'.

This class performs all the extraction of information from the log file.
For efficiency, the instances contain the whole file as a list of strings
so that it can be read several times with no disk access.

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.SourceParser
documentation:
Extends the general-purpose TeX parser to handle Rubber directives in the
comment lines.

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.EndDocument
documentation: This is the exception raised when \end{document} is found.

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.EndInput
documentation: This is the exception raised when \endinput is found.

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the environment. This prepares the processing steps for the
given file (all steps are initialized empty) and sets the regular
expressions and the hook dictionary.
--  Initialization  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.set_source
documentation:
Specify the main source for the document. The exact path and file name
are determined, and the source building process is updated if needed,
according the the source file's extension. The optional argument
'jobname' can be used to specify the job name to something else that
the base of the file name.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.set_primary_product_suffix
documentation: Change the suffix of the primary product

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.new_aux_file
documentation: Register a new latex .aux file

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.includeonly
documentation:
Use partial compilation, by appending a call to \includeonly on the
command line on compilation.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.source
documentation: Return the main source's complete filename.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.parse
documentation: Parse the source for packages and supported macros.
--  LaTeX source parsing  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.parse_file
documentation:
Process a LaTeX source. The file must be open, it is read to the end
calling the handlers for the macro calls. This recursively processes
the included sources.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.process
documentation:
This method is called when an included file is processed. The argument
must be a valid file name.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.input_file
documentation:
Treat the given name as a source file to be read. If this source can
be the result of some conversion, then the conversion is performed,
otherwise the source is parsed. The returned value is a couple
(name,dep) where `name' is the actual LaTeX source and `dep' is
its dependency node. The return value is (None,None) if the source
could neither be read nor built.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.command
documentation:
Execute the rubber command 'cmd' with arguments 'args'. This is called
when a command is found in the source file or in a configuration file.
A command name of the form 'foo.bar' is considered to be a command
'bar' for module 'foo'. The argument 'pos' describes the position
(file and line) where the command occurs.
--  Directives  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.hook_macro
documentation: --  Macro handling  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_begin
documentation: Now the macro handlers:

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_pdfoutput
documentation:
Called when \pdfoutput is found. Tries to guess if it is a definition
that asks for the output to be in PDF or DVI.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_input
documentation:
Called when an \input macro is found. This calls the `process' method
if the included file is found.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_include
documentation:
Called when an \include macro is found. This includes files into the
source in a way very similar to \input, except that LaTeX also
creates .aux files for them, so we have to notice this.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_includeonly
documentation:
Called when the macro \includeonly is found, indicates the
comma-separated list of files that should be included, so that the
othe \include are ignored.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_documentclass
documentation:
Called when the macro \documentclass is found. It almost has the same
effect as `usepackage': if the source's directory contains the class
file, in which case this file is treated as an input, otherwise a
module is searched for to support the class.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_usepackage
documentation:
Called when a \usepackage macro is found. If there is a package in the
directory of the source file, then it is treated as an include file
unless there is a supporting module in the current directory,
otherwise it is treated as a package.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_bibliography
documentation:
Called when the macro \bibliography is found. This method actually
registers the module bibtex (if not already done) and registers the
databases.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_bibliographystyle
documentation:
Called when \bibliographystyle is found. This registers the module
bibtex (if not already done) and calls the method set_style() of the
module.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_begin_verbatim
documentation:
Called when \begin{verbatim} is found. This disables all macro
handling and comment parsing until the end of the environment. The
optional argument 'end' specifies the end marker, by default it is
"\end{verbatim}".

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_endinput
documentation:
Called when \endinput is found. This stops the processing of the
current input file, thus ignoring any code that appears afterwards.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.h_end_document
documentation:
Called when \end{document} is found. This stops the processing of any
input file, thus ignoring any code that appears afterwards.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.compile
documentation:
Run one LaTeX compilation on the source. Return true on success or
false if errors occured.
--  Compilation steps  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.pre_compile
documentation:
Prepare the source for compilation using package-specific functions.
This function must return False on failure.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.post_compile
documentation:
Run the package-specific operations that are to be performed after
each compilation of the main source. Returns true on success or false
on failure.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.clean
documentation: Remove all files that are produced by compilation.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.run
documentation:
Run the building process until the last compilation, or stop on error.
This method supposes that the inputs were parsed to register packages
and that the LaTeX source is ready. If the second (optional) argument
is true, then at least one compilation is done. As specified by the
parent class, the method returns True on success and False on
failure.
--  Building routine  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.get_errors
documentation: --  Utility methods  {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.watch_file
documentation:
Register the given file (typically "jobname.toc" or such) to be
watched. When the file changes during a compilation, it means that
another compilation has to be done.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.LaTeXDep.remove_suffixes
documentation:
Remove all files derived from the main source with one of the
specified suffixes.

This class represents dependency nodes for LaTeX compilation. It handles
the cyclic LaTeX compilation until a stable output, including actual
compilation (with a parametrable executable) and possible processing of
compilation results (e.g. running BibTeX).

Before building (or cleaning) the document, the method `parse' must be
called to load and configure all required modules. Text lines are read
from the files and parsed to extract LaTeX macro calls. When such a macro
is found, a handler is searched for in the `hooks' dictionary. Handlers
are called with one argument: the dictionary for the regular expression
that matches the macro call.

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex-1.5.1.ScriptModule
documentation: This class represents modules that are defined as Rubber scripts.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.graphicx
documentation:
Support for the `graphics' package in Rubber.

This package is destined de become something rather large, to support standard
schemes for generating the figures that are supposed to be included, among
other features.

Another feature to include in the future concerns the parsing of the commands
that customize the operation of the package, such as \DeclareGraphicsRule.

Currently, only dependency analysis is provided. The command parsing is
incomplete, because \includegraphics can have a complex format.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

methods.name: rubber.latex_modules.graphicx.Module.hook_includegraphics
documentation: Supported macros

methods.name: rubber.latex_modules.graphicx.Module.pre_compile
documentation: module interface

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.minitoc
documentation:
Support for the minitoc package.

This package allows for the insertion of partial tables of contents at the
level of chapters, parts and sections. This nice feature has the drawback of
producing a lot of auxiliary files, and this module handles the cleaning of
these.

It relies on the listfiles option, wich is active by default. Listing
the produced files is quite complex (see shortext option for example).
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

methods.name: rubber.latex_modules.minitoc.Module.clean
documentation: Adding the .maf file to products may lead it to be removed
before we can read it. It is wise to deal with it locally.

modules.name: rubber.converters.latex
documentation:
LaTeX document building system for Rubber.

This module contains all the code in Rubber that actually does the job of
building a LaTeX document from start to finish.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
(c) Sebastian Kapfer 2015
vim: noet:ts=4

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex.Modules

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.Modules.__getitem__
documentation: Return the module object of the given name.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.Modules.__contains__
documentation: Check if a given module is loaded.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.Modules.register
documentation:
Attempt to register a module with the specified name. If the module is
already loaded, do nothing. If it is found and not yet loaded, then
load it, initialise it (using the opt passed as optional argument)
and run any delayed commands for it.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.Modules.command
documentation:
Send a command to a particular module. If this module is not loaded,
store the command so that it will be sent when the module is registered.

This class gathers all operations related to the management of modules.
The modules are    searched for first in the current directory, then as
scripts in the 'modules' directory in the program's data directory, then
as a Python module in the package `rubber.latex'.
----  Module handler  ----{{{1

classes.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.__init__
documentation: -- Initialization {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.readlog
documentation:
Read the specified log file, checking that it was produced by the
right compiler. Returns False if the log file is invalid or does not
exist.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.errors
documentation: Returns true if there was an error during the compilation.
-- Process information {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.continued
documentation:
Check if a line in the log is continued on the next line. This is
needed because TeX breaks messages at 79 characters per line. We make
this into a method because the test is slightly different in Metapost.
-- Information extraction {{{2

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.parse
documentation:
Parse the log file for relevant information. The named arguments are
booleans that indicate which information should be extracted:
- errors: all errors
- boxes: bad boxes
- refs: warnings about references
- warnings: all other warnings
The function returns a generator. Each generated item is a dictionary
that contains (some of) the following entries:
- kind: the kind of information ("error", "box", "ref", "warning")
- text: the text of the error or warning
- code: the piece of code that caused an error
- file, line, last, pkg: as used by Message.format_pos.

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.update_file
documentation:
Parse the given line of log file for file openings and closings and
update the list `stack'. Newly opened files are at the end, therefore
stack[1] is the main source while stack[-1] is the current one. The
first element, stack[0], contains the value None for errors that may
happen outside the source. Return the last file from which text was
read (the new stack top, or the one before the last closing
parenthesis).

methods.name: rubber.converters.latex.LogCheck.update_page
documentation:
Parse the given line and return the number of the page that is being
built after that line, assuming the current page before the line was
`before'.

This class performs all the extraction of information from the log file.
For efficiency, the instances contain the whole file as a list of strings
so that it can be read several times with no disk access.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.bibtopic
documentation:
Support for the 'bibtopic' LaTeX package.

Each 'btSect' environment generates separate .aux and .bbl files.
The .aux must then be processed by 'bibtex' to produce a more
interesting .bbl, and also a .bbl log.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
bibtopic.py, part of Rubber building system for LaTeX documents..

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.asymptote
documentation:
This module supports the 'asymptote' LaTeX package.

The content of each 'asy' environment is copied into a separate .asy
file, which must then be processed by the 'asy' external tool to
produce a .eps/.pdf.  If the output already exists, it is inserted,
but a (maybe identical) .asy is created nevertheless.

The inline option may be passed with \usepackage[inline]{asymptote} or
 egin[inline]{asy}, and asks 'asy' to produce and insert TeX output
instead of .eps/.pdf. As rubber currently fails to report environment
options, only the former is recognized.

Asymptote insists on replacing the main .aux file with an empty one,
so we backup its content before running the external tool.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
asymptote.py, part of Rubber building system for LaTeX documents..
Copyright (C) 2015-2015 Nicolas Boulenguez <nicolas.boulenguez@free.fr>
This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify
it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
the Free Software Foundation; either version 2 of the License, or
(at your option) any later version.
This program is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
GNU General Public License for more details.
You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
along with this program.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.

functions.name: rubber.latex_modules.asymptote.inline_option
documentation: Returns None if inline is unset, else a boolean.

classes.name: rubber.latex_modules.asymptote.Shell_Restoring_Aux
documentation:
This class replaces Shell because of a bug in asymptote. Every run
of /usr/bin/asy flushes the .aux file.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.bibtex
documentation:
BibTeX support for Rubber

This module is a special one: it is triggered by the macros \bibliography and
\bibliographystyle and not as a package, so the main system knows about it.
The module provides the following commands:

  crossrefs FIXME
  path <dir> = adds <dir> to the search path for databases
  stylepath <dir> = adds <dir> to the search path for styles
  tool FIXME
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

documentation:
Support for the minitoc package.

This package allows for the insertion of partial tables of contents at the
level of chapters, parts and sections. This nice feature has the drawback of
producing a lot of auxiliary files, and this module handles the cleaning of
these.

It relies on the listfiles option, wich is active by default. Listing
the produced files is quite complex (see shortext option for example).
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

methods.name: rubber.latex_modules.minitoc-hyper.Module.clean
documentation: Adding the .maf file to products may lead it to be removed
before we can read it. It is wise to deal with it locally.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.epsfig
documentation:
Epsfig support for Rubber.

The package 'epsfig' is a somewhat deprecated interface to the graphics module
that simply provides a macro \psfig with a keyval list argument to include
an EPS figure file.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2003--2006

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.nomencl
documentation:
Support for nomenclatures with package 'nomencl'.

This module simply wraps the functionality of the 'index' module with default
values for the 'nomencl' package.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.vtex
documentation:
VTeX support for Rubber.

This module specifies that the VTeX/Free compiler should be used. This
includes using "vlatex" of "vlatexp" instead of "latex" and knowing that this
produces a PDF or PostScript file directly. The PDF version is used by
default, switching to PS is possible by using the module option "ps".
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

documentation: SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.xr
documentation:
Dependency analysis for the xr package.

The xr package allows one to put references in one document to other
(external) LaTeX documents. It works by reading the external document's .aux
file, so this support package registers these files as dependencies.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006

based on code by Sebastian Reichel and others.

functions.name: rubber.cmdline.prepare_source
documentation:
Prepare the dependency node for the main LaTeX run.
Returns the filename of the main LaTeX source file, which might
change for various reasons (adding a .tex suffix; preprocessors;
pipe dumping).
When this is done, the file must exist on disk, otherwise this
function must exit(1) or exit(2).

functions.name: rubber.cmdline.build
documentation: Build the final product.

functions.name: rubber.cmdline.display
documentation:
Print an error or warning message. The argument 'kind' indicates the
kind of message, among "error", "warning", "abort", the argument
'text' is the main text of the message, the other arguments provide
additional information, including the location of the error.

functions.name: rubber.cmdline.prepare_source_pipe
documentation:
Dump the standard input in a file, and set up that file
the same way we would normally process LaTeX sources.

functions.name: rubber.cmdline.process_source_pipe
documentation: Build the document, and dump the result on stdout.

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.combine
documentation:
Support for package 'combine' in Rubber.

This package is used to gather several complete LaTeX documents into a single
one, possibly fine-tuning page numbering, generation of tables of contents,
and other features. Documents are included using the macro \import, so
basically this support module makes it behave somewhat like \include.
However, more subtle behaviour could be implemented, e.g. to reflect the the
way the package deals with \documentclass and \usepackage.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2003--2006

classes.name: rubber.latex_modules.combine.Module
documentation: FIXME: should add the appropriate dependency nodes for the new .aux
.log .tex files, or a new LatexDep node, or ..?

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.multibib
documentation:
Multibib support for Rubber

This package allows several bibliographies in one document. Each occurence of
the \newcites macro creates a new bibliography with its associated commands,
using a new aux file. This modules behaves like the default BibTeX module for
each of those files.

The directives are the same as those of the BibTeX module. They all accept an
optional argument first, enclosed in parentheses as in "multibib.path
(foo,bar) here/", to specify which bibliography they apply to. Without this
argument, they apply to all bibliographies.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2004--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

modules.name: rubber.latex_modules.index
documentation:
Indexing support with package 'index'.

This module handles the processing of the document's indices using a tool like
makeindex or xindy. It stores an MD5 sum of the source (.idx) file between two
runs, in order to detect modifications.

The following directives are provided to specify options for makeindex:

  tool <tool> =
    Choose which indexing tool should be used. Currently this can be either
    "makeindex" (by default) or "xindy".

  language <lang> =
    Choose the language used for sorting the index (xindy only).

  modules <mod> <mod> ... =
      Specify which modules xindy should use for the index.

  order <ordering> =
    Modify the ordering to be used (makeindex only, supported by xindy with
    warnings). The argument must be a space separated list of:
    - standard = use default ordering (no options, this is the default)
    - german = use German ordering (option "-g")
    - letter = use letter instead of word ordering (option "-l")

  path <directory> =
    Add the specified directory to the search path for styles.

  style <name> =
    Use the specified style file.

They all accept an optional argument first, enclosed in parentheses as in
"index.path (foo,bar) here/", to specify which index they apply to. Without
this argument, they apply to all indices declared at the point where they
occur.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2004--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

methods.name: rubber.latex_modules.index.Module.register
documentation: Register a new index.

modules.name: rubber.contents
documentation: SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later

functions.name: rubber.contents.snapshot
documentation:
A snapshot of the contents of an external file.

The special value NO_SUCH_FILE is returned when path does not
refer to an existing external file. However, an exception is
raised if an existing file vanishes between two calls.

The implementation trusts the operating system about modification
times, and assumes that an unchanged time stamp implies unchanged
contents. Malicious or unadvised users may change timestamps.

Moreover, an overwrite will not be detected if it is more recent
than the smallest interval representable by operating timestamps.

The implementation relies on the MD5 hash to detect modified
contents. For such a non-cryptographic use,  the probability of
collision (2^-64) can be neglected for all practical needs.

modules.name: rubber.environment
documentation:
This module contains the code for
the class Environment, which contains all information about a given
building process.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2002--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

classes.name: rubber.environment.Environment

methods.name: rubber.environment.Environment.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the environment. The optional argument is the path to the
reference directory for compilation, by default it is the current
working directory.

methods.name: rubber.environment.Environment.find_file
documentation:
Look for a source file with the given name, and return either the
complete path to the actual file or None if the file is not found.
The optional argument is a suffix that may be added to the name.

methods.name: rubber.environment.Environment.conv_set
documentation:
Define preferences for the generation of a given file. The argument
'file' is the name of the target and the argument 'vars' is a
dictionary that contains imposed values for some variables.

methods.name: rubber.environment.Environment.convert
documentation:
Use conversion rules to make a given target file, and return:
* the file path of an existing file matching the 'target' pattern
* the final node of a new dependency tree if a conversion is necessary
* None if the file does not exist and cannot be built.
The optional arguments 'prefixes' and 'suffixes'
are lists of strings that can be added at the beginning and the end of
the name when searching for the file. Prefixes are tried in order, and
for each prefix, suffixes are tried in order; the first file from this
order that exists or can be made is kept. The optional arguments
'check' and 'context' have the same meaning as in
'Converter.best_rule'.

methods.name: rubber.environment.Environment.may_produce
documentation:
Return true if the given filename may be that of a file generated by
any of the converters.

This class contains all state information related to the building process
for a whole document, the dependency graph and conversion rules.

modules.name: rubber.index
documentation: Common support for makeindex and xindy external tools.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later
(c) Emmanuel Beffara, 2004--2006
vim: noet:ts=4

classes.name: rubber.index.Index

methods.name: rubber.index.Index.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the index, by specifying the source file (generated by
LaTeX), the target file (the output of makeindex) and the transcript
(e.g. .ilg) file.  Transcript is used by glosstex.py.
This class represents a single index.
FIXME: this class may probably be simplified a lot if inheriting
from rubber.depend.Shell instead of rubber.depend.Node.

modules.name: rubber.convert
documentation:
This module contains code for file conversion, including implicit conversion
rule management.
SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later

functions.name: rubber.convert.expand_cases
documentation:
Expand variables and cases in a template string. Variables must occur as
$VAR (with only letters in the name) or ${VAR}, their values are taken
from the dictionary-like object 'vars'. The "$" character can be written
litterally by saying "$$". Cases are written in braces, separated with
commas, as in {foo,bar,quux}. Commas at top-level also count as choice
separators, as if there was a pair of braces around the whole string.
Returns a pair (cases,pos) where 'cases' is the list of expansions of the
string and 'pos' is the position of the first unbalanced "}" or the end of
the string.

classes.name: rubber.convert.Converter

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the converter, associated with a given dependency set, with
an empty set of rules.

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.read_ini
documentation:
Read a set of rules from a file. The file has the form of an INI file,
each section describes a rule.

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.load_module
documentation:
Check if the module of the given name exists and load it. Returns True
if the module was loaded and False otherwise.

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.may_produce
documentation:
Return true if the given filename may be that of a file generated by
this converter, i.e. if it matches one of the target regular
expressions.

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.best_rule
documentation:
Search for an applicable rule for the given target with the least
cost. The returned value is a dictionary that describes the best rule
found, or None if no rule is applicable. The optional argument 'check'
is a function that takes the rule parameters as arguments (as a
dictionary that contains at least 'source' and 'target') and can
return false if the rule is refused. The optional argument 'context'
is expected to be a Variables instance attached to a Node.

methods.name: rubber.convert.Converter.apply
documentation:
Apply a rule with the variables given in the dictionary passed as
argument (as returned from the 'best_rule' method), and return a
dependency node for the result.

This class represents a set of translation rules that may be used to
produce input files. Objects contain a table of rewriting rules to deduce
potential source names from the target's name, and each rule has a given
cost that indicates how expensive the translation is.

Each rule contains a module name. The module is searched for in the
package rubber.converters and it is supposed to contain two functions:

- check(source, target, context):
    Returns True if conversion from 'source' to 'target' is possible (i.e.
    the source file is suitable, all required tools are available, etc.).
    The 'context' object is a dictionary-like object that contains values
    from the rule and possibly additional user settings. If the function
    is absent, conversion is always assumed to be possible.

- convert(source, target, context, env):
    Produce a dependency node to produce 'target' from
    'source', using settings from the environment 'env'.
